name: Galápagos Tortoise
summary: Galápagos tortoises are enormous island reptiles with heavy shells, long lives, and a calm, slow way of moving. They graze on grasses and cactus across the Galápagos Islands and are among the biggest tortoises in the world.
metaTitle: Galápagos Tortoise Facts | Diet, Lifespan & Photos
metaDescription: Discover Galápagos tortoise facts with photos, island habitat, plant diet, hatchlings, shell types, and conservation.
searchIntents: how long do galapagos tortoises live
how big is a galapagos tortoise

lifespan.captivity: More than 150 years in human care

reproduction.gestationOrIncubation: Eggs incubate in warm soil for about 4 to 8 months

funFacts: Galápagos tortoises can live longer than many people.
Different island tortoises can have dome-shaped or saddleback shells.
They help spread plant seeds across the islands in their droppings.
A Galápagos tortoise can go a very long time without food or fresh water.
These giant reptiles often cool off in muddy wallows.
Some tortoise populations have recovered because of captive breeding and island restoration.

faq.question: What is a Galápagos tortoise?
faq.answer: A Galápagos tortoise is a giant tortoise native to the Galápagos Islands. It is one of the largest tortoises alive today.

faq.question: Where do Galápagos tortoises live?
faq.answer: They live only on the Galápagos Islands, an Ecuadorian archipelago in the Pacific Ocean west of South America.

faq.question: What do Galápagos tortoises eat?
faq.answer: They are herbivores that eat grasses, leaves, cactus pads, fruit, and other island plants.

faq.question: How long do Galápagos tortoises live?
faq.answer: Many live 80 years or more in the wild, and some in human care have lived well past 100 years.

faq.question: Why do some Galápagos tortoises have different shell shapes?
faq.answer: Shell shapes vary by island habitat. Saddleback shells help tortoises stretch higher for food, while dome shells suit places with more low plants.

faq.question: Are Galápagos tortoises endangered?
faq.answer: Many Galápagos tortoise populations were badly reduced in the past. Protection and breeding programs have helped some island groups recover, but they still need conservation.
